--- 
always_allow_html: true
text-align: center
fontsize: 12pt
title: SCoRe. Checklist for conducting and reporting an empirical NCA study 
author: | 
  | Jan Dul
date: |
  | Last update: `r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y'`)</font>
  | 
  | Suggested reference: Dul, J. (2021). Necessary Condition Analysis. Principles and Application. Chapman & Hall, CRC Press.
output:
  html_document: default
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
linestretch: 1
toc-depth: 3
lof: FALSE
lot: FALSE
documentclass: book
classoption: oneside
bibliography: [nca4g.bib]
link-citations: yes
cover-image: 
---
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.datatables.net/2.1.8/css/dataTables.dataTables.css" />
<script src="https://cdn.datatables.net/2.1.8/js/dataTables.js"></script>

<style>
.inline-table {
  width: 1000px;
}

.table-container {
    max-width: 100%;
}

#assesments td {
    font-size: 10px;
}

/* Adjust column widths */
#assesments th:nth-child(1), #assesments td:nth-child(1) { width: 10%; } /* Section */
#assesments th:nth-child(2), #assesments td:nth-child(2) { width: 10%; } /* Priority */
#assesments th:nth-child(3), #assesments td:nth-child(3) { width: 5%; } /* Item */
#assesments th:nth-child(4), #assesments td:nth-child(4) { width: 25%; } /* Question */
#assesments th:nth-child(5), #assesments td:nth-child(5) { width: 40%; } /* Recommendation */
#assesments th:nth-child(6), #assesments td:nth-child(6) { width: 10%; } /* Further Reading */


</style>

<table class="inputs">
    <tbody>
        <tr>
            <td><label for="publication">Publication section:</label></td>
            <td>
                <select name="publication" id="publication" multiple>
                  <option value="All" selected>All</option>
                  <option value="Introduction/General">Introduction/General</option>
                  <option value="Theory/Hypotheses">Theory/Hypotheses</option>
                  <option value="Methods - data">Methods - data</option>
                  <option value="Methods - data analysis">Methods - data analysis</option>
                  <option value="Results">Results</option>
                  <option value="Discussion">Discussion</option>
                </select>
            </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <td><label for="priority">Priority:</label></td>
            <td>
                <select name="priority" id="priority" multiple>
                  <option value="All" selected>All</option>
                  <option value="Must-have">Must-have</option>
                  <option value="Should-have">Should-have</option>
                  <option value="Nice-to-have">Nice-to-have</option>
                </select>
            </td>
        </tr>
    </tbody>
</table>

<div style="overflow-x: auto; overflow-y: auto; max-height: 600px; border: 1px solid #ddd;">
<table id="assesments" class="display" style="width:100%">
    <thead>
        <tr>
            <th>Section</th>
            <th>Priority</th>
            <th>Item</th>
            <th>Question</th>
            <th>Recommendation</th>
            <th>Further reading</th>
        </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>

<!-- INTRODUCTION -->
<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 1</td><td>Are the goals and contributions of applying NCA explicitly stated?
</td><td>State the primary and (possibly) secondairy goals and contributions of the empirical NCA study. The study may primarily contribute to theory development by, for example, developing and testing new necessity hypotheses, developing an existing necessity claims further towards a formal necessity hypotheses and testing it with NCA, or replication of results of previously tested necessity hypotheses. Secondary goals could be methodological (e.g., introducing NCA to a new field/phenomenon), or practical (e.g., using results of NCA to produce practically meaningful recommendations/insights).
</td><td>Articles: @bokrantz2022building; @dul2024different; @hollenbeck2017harking; @kohler2023constructive; @bergh2022constitutes; This book: Sections  \@ref(theoreticalcontribution) and \@ref(relatedmultiple)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 2</td><td>When referring to necessity, are only words that correctly describe a necessity relationship used?</td><td>Refer to necessity only by using words that describe a necessity relationship. Avoid imprecise descriptions like X (cor)relates with Y, X is associated with Y, X affects Y, X causes Y, etc. Avoid incorrect sufficiency-based descriptions like X produces Y, X drives Y, X increase/decreases Y, etc.</td><td>This book: Table \@ref(tab:wordsnecessity)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 3</td><td>Is it explained that NCA is an approach that combines both a specific causal logic (methodology) and the related data analysis technique (method)?</td><td>Refer to NCA as an approach that combines necessity causality (rather than common probabilistic or configurational causality) and a related data analysis technique (ensuring theory-method fit). NCA is not just a data analysis technique or statistical tool.</td><td>Article: @dul2024different</td></tr>

<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 4</td><td>Is a proper comparison made between NCA's causal perspective and conventional causal perspectives (e.g., causal inference from regression analysis or QCA studies)?</td><td>When comparing necessity (causal) logic and NCA with causal interpretations of conventional regression-based/statistical approaches, use the term “probabilistic sufficiency” and not just “sufficiency” for the latter approaches, as &quot;sufficiency&quot; suggests (quasi-)determinism, like in QCA configurational sufficiency logic. This recommendation is often violated in studies that combine NCA with PLS-SEM. When NCA is compared with QCA's necessity analysis, use the term &quot;necessity analysis of QCA&quot; for the latter (and not &quot;NCA&quot;) and explain the differences between the necessity analysis of QCA (only in kind) and that of NCA (also in degree).</td><td>Articles: @dul2024different; @dul2016jbr; @vis2018analyzing; @dul2022problematic; This book: Section \@ref(ncaplssem); Section \@ref(ncaqca)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 5</td><td>In multimethod studies, is NCA used as an approach with a different perspective on causality and data analysis (and not as a robustness or confirmation test for other methods), and vice versa?</td><td>Use NCA as an approach with a different perspective on causality and therefore a different way of data analysis. NCA is not a robustness or a confirmation test for other methods (and vice versa).</td><td>Article: @dul2024different</td></tr>

<tr><td>Introduction/General</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 6</td><td>If the necessity analysis is a prominent part of the publication, do the title and abstract reflect this necessity perspective/NCA?</td><td>If an important part of the publication and/or its conclusions are based on necessity logic and NCA, include in the title (and abstract) words that reflect necessity logic or NCA.</td><td>This book: Table \@ref(tab:wordsnecessity)</td></tr>

<!-- THEORY -->
<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 7</td><td>Is the hypothesized necessity relationship explicitly formulated as: X is necessary for Y, or similar?</td><td>Formulate the necessity relationship explicitly as a necessary condition hypothesis: &quot;X is necessary for Y&quot;. For hypothesis testing research this is done before data analysis; for hypothesis building/exploration research this is done after data analysis when a necessity relationship is found.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(formulationnecessity) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 8</td><td>Are the four elements of a necessity theory precisely defined (condition(s) X, outcome Y, direction of the hypothesis, focal unit, theoretical domain)?</td><td>Specify the theoretical domain of the necessity theory/hypothesis (theoretical phenomenon/constructs for which the necessity relation holds), and the focal unit of analysis (the population, or subset of population, of focal units). Note that the theoretical domain is usually larger than a population for empirical study, and the theoretical domain may be better specified after doing a thought experiment.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(partstheory) and \@ref(thoughtexperiment)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 9</td><td>Is it convincingly explained WHY X is necessary for Y (no compensation possible), using necessity causal logic to justify the necessity hypothesis?</td><td>Explain why X is necessary for Y using necessity logic (why compensation is not possible) based on experience of academics and practitioners, and/or literature and experiences of academics and practitioners.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(thoughtexperiment) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 10</td><td>Are temporal aspects considered including specifying the expected direction of the temporal order of X and Y if this is not obvious?</td><td>If this is not obvious, include a plausible explanation why the temporal order is X before Y (why Y cannot happen without X having occurred) and why the alternative explanation, &quot;Y is sufficient for X&quot; is not plausible.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(temporalorder)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 11</td><td>Is the existing literature about the relationship between X and Y re-considered from a necessity causal perspective?</td><td>Review existing literature (that commonly describes relationships in sufficiency terms) about the relationship between X and Y and interpret/re-consider this literature from a necessity causal perspective. This helps to see if the relationship might actually be a necessity relationship, and might also provide arguments (based on previous literature) for making a justification for necessity causal logic.</td><td>This book: Table \@ref(tab:wordsnecessity)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 12</td><td>If applicable, is an &quot;nc&quot; symbol added near the arrow in a figure that represents a necessity relationship?</td><td>In a figure representing an (expected) necessity relationship, add a small symbol (nc, nc- or +nc-) near the arrow to show the direction of necessity.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(direction)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Theory/Hypotheses</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 13</td><td>Is it specified if rare exceptions are allowed?</td><td>When formulating necessity theory/hypotheses explicitly state if rare exceptions are allowed (probabilistic perspective on necessity, &quot;almost always necessary’&quot;) or not (deterministic perspective on necessity).</td><td>Article: @dul2024different</td></tr>

<!-- METHODS-DATA -->
<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 14</td><td>Is the study design (observational study, longitudinal study, case study, experimental study) adequate?</td><td>Specify: 1. the research design which can be the experiment (when there is theoretical, methodological and practical possibility of manipulating X and having a control group), observational study (cross-sectional, longitudinal), qualitative/case study, simulation study, or other; 2. the data set and type of data (quantitative, qualitative, simulated). Note: NCA is a method that analyzes data without generating data. NCA is not a method for collecting data. NCA can analyze all types of data. However, due to the specific causal logic, NCA makes specific requirements to data. These requirements depend on the research design, and include requirements for sample size, sampling, measurement, and distribution characteristics of X and Y (boundedness, ceiling). Specify how the research design meets requirements for the levels of X and Y. Data are input to NCA, not part of NCA.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(experiment), \@ref(samplesize), \@ref(qualitative), \@ref(longitudinal), and \@ref(setmembership)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 15</td><td>For large N quantitative studies: are adequate sampling approaches used and is a pre-study power analysis reported?</td><td>For large N quantitative studies, use sampling approaches that are appropriate for identifying empty zones in scatter plots and for accurately estimating ceilings. Thus: use random sampling and sample size should be sufficiently large (rule of thumb: N&gt;=100). For testing necessity hypotheses, power analysis is needed. Provide a pre-study power analysis for effect size (d) and statistical significance (p). Post-hoc power analysis is not informative for the present study but could inform future studies only.</td><td>Article: @dul2024how; This book: Section \@ref(power)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 16</td><td>For small N qualitative studies: are adequate NCA-specific approaches used for purposive case selection?</td><td>For qualitative small N studies, use NCA-specific purposive case selection approaches. If using typical qualitative purposeful case selection approaches, specify the type of case selection and its limitations.</td><td>Article: @dul2024how; This book: Section \@ref(smalln)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 17</td><td>Are adequate measurement approaches used for getting scores of X and Y, and are these measures practically interpretable?</td><td>Use measurement approaches that make the scores of X and Y practically interpretable, i.e. meaningfully reflect the level of X and Y (not just &quot;rank&quot;). NCA is a data analytic technique applied to measures of X and Y. Data are input to NCA, not part of NCA.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(experiment), \@ref(samplesize), \@ref(qualitative), \@ref(longitudinal), and \@ref(setmembership)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 18</td><td>For simulation studies: are simulated data sampled from bounded distributions of X and Y?</td><td>NCA requires bounded distributions for both X and Y (e.g. physical constraints, or scale boundedness). For simulation studies, sample data from bounded distributions. Uniform distribution or bounded beta are eligible but not sampling from normal distributed data.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(requirementssim)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 19</td><td>Are only data transformations that align with NCA employed?</td><td>Explain possible data transformations. NCA only allows linear transformations of X and Y (affine transformations: X2=aX+b) because necessity relationship depends on absolute levels of X and Y. Log and other non-linear transformations are not allowed. Exception: piecewise linear transformation (called 'S-curve') is applied mechanistically for calibration purposes.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(transformation) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<!-- METHODS - DATA ANALYSIS -->
<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 20</td><td>Is the selected ceiling line specified and justified?</td><td>Specify and justify the primary selection of the ceiling line (e.g., CE-FDH when X or Y is discrete or when the ceiling line is expected to be non-linear; CR-FDH when X and Y are continuous or when the ceiling line is expected to be straight).</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(selectioneilingline), \@ref(choiceceiling), and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 21</td><td>Is the selected scope (empirical or theoretical) deliberately selected and specified?</td><td></td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 22</td><td>Are the hypothesis evaluation criteria (thresholds for effect size, statistical significance, ceiling fit, ceiling accuracy, and possibly target outcome) deliberately specified and justified?</td><td>Specify and justify the selection of the threshold value for necessity effect size for in kind necessity (d) and for statistical significance (p). In absence of a specified value, a common benchmark value may be used (d = 0.10).</td><td>Article: @dul2016orm; This book: Sections \@ref(inkind) and \@ref(choiceeffectsize)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 23</td><td>Are the results of a visual inspection of the XY-plot reported?</td><td></td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 24</td><td>Is it explained how potential outliers are analysed and handled?</td><td></td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 25</td><td>Is it described and justified how the ceiling line estimation accuracy is evaluated?</td><td>Specify how ceiling line accuracy is evaluated and which threshold values are used; consider for example Fit &gt; 80% and ceiling-accuracy &gt; 0.95</td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 26</td><td>Are all NCA-related parameters, terms and concepts properly used and described?</td><td>Make sure that the feasible area is described as the area under the ceiling line and not as the empty zone. Avoid using the term &quot;ceiling zone&quot; and &quot;infeasible area&quot;. Provide a proper description of effect size, ceiling line, accuracy, and scope. Specify what is meant by the data-analytic term &quot;relative&quot; (relative to observed scores; relative to selected scale for scoring X and Y; comparing effect sizes as in simulations).</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(formalexpression)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 27</td><td>Is the utilized software, including its version number specified?</td><td>Specify which software and which version of the software was used to conduct NCA.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(software)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Methods - data analysis</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 28</td><td>Is a short description of NCA’s data analysis approach provided?</td><td>Provide to readers who are unfamiliar with NCA’s data analysis approach a short description of how NCA works (e.g., focusing on empty zone, ceiling line, scope, effect size) and refer to references for further reading.</td><td>This book: Chapter \@ref(summary) ; Appendix in @dul2024different</td></tr>

<!-- RESULTS -->
<tr><td>Results</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 29</td><td>Are XY-tables or XY-plots of all tested/explored relationships shown?</td><td>Show in the main text or an appendix the XY tables or XY plots of all tested/explored relationships such that the reader can inspect the patterns.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(visualinspection) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 30</td><td>Is only the ceiling line that is used to draw conclusions about the necessity relationship shown in the XY-plot?</td><td>Report results of another plausible ceiling line if this is part of a robustness check</td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 31</td><td>For large-N quantitative research: are the effect size and its p-value properly reported?</td><td>Report the estimated effect size preferably with two decimal places (e.g., d = 0.09 or d = 0.11), ensuring that the two decimal p values have meaningful information about the precision of the p value.</td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 32</td><td>Is the conclusion about necessity in kind based on three criteria: theoretical justification, effect size, and p-value?</td><td>Conclude about necessity in kind using three criteria: 1. theoretical justification (the hypothesis); 2. Effect size large enough (above the selected threshold value), p value small enough (below the selected threshold value).</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(summarybottleneck) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 33</td><td>Is the bottleneck table reported and necessity in degree evaluated?</td><td>Report the bottleneck table when multiple necessary conditions are not rejected to evaluate for which level of Y, which levels of the X&#x27;s are required.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(bottleneck)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Nice-to-have</td><td> 34</td><td>If applicable: is the meaning of &#x27;NN&#x27; and &#x27;NA&#x27; in the bottleneck table explained?</td><td>Explain the meaning of &#x27;NN&#x27; and &#x27;NA&#x27; in the bottleneck table.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(bottleneck)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 35</td><td>Are the type of values used in the bottleneck analysis justified?</td><td>Justify the type of values for X and Y (percentage of range, actual values, percentiles). In particular, the actual values of X and Y are meaningful, and not the percentage of cases not able to achieve a target outcome.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(bottleneck)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Results</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 36</td><td>Are the results of the robustness checks summarized?</td><td>Summarize the results of the robustness checks: is the conclusion about necessity (e.g., acceptance and rejection of hypotheses, effect sizes) robust across choice of ceiling line (e.g., CE-FDH, CR-FDH), scope (observed, theoretical), removing outliers and missing values, and other choices (e.g., scope outliers). Conclude if the results are robust or fragile.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(robustnesschecks)</td></tr>

<!-- DISCUSSION -->
<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 37</td><td>Is it explained why all tested necessary conditions were (not) rejected?</td><td>Summarize the results of using NCA in terms of which necessary conditions were tested/explored, and which were (not) rejected. Explain what could be a theoretical reason of it (refer to the reasoning for the hypothesis formulation).</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(ncaqca)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 38</td><td>For multimethod research: is it explained how the results of NCA and of other methods complement each other?</td><td>When NCA is used in combination with another methods (e.g., regression-based methods or QCA), report how the results of NCA and of the other method complement each other.</td><td>This book: Sections \@ref(ncaregression) and \@ref(ncaplssem)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Must-have</td><td> 39</td><td>Referring to the goals of the study (see introduction): is it discussed if the intended contribution is realized and what the key insights are?</td><td>For a theoretical, empirical, methodological and/or practical contribution (see introduction), discuss if the added value has been realized and what key insights were gained.</td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 40</td><td>Are the results of the bottleneck analysis explained in terms of necessity-in-degree?</td><td>Give an interpretation of necessity in degree using the results of the bottleneck analysis. For example, explain what level of X is necessary for what target level of Y.</td><td>This book: Section \@ref(bottleneck)</td></tr>

<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 41</td><td>Are limitations of applying NCA mentioned?</td><td>Mention general limitations of NCA (e.g., only focus on necessity; potential sensitivity to outliers), and specific limitations of applying NCA in the current study.</td><td></td></tr>

<tr><td>Discussion</td><td>Should-have</td><td> 42</td><td>Are potential future studies with NCA discussed?</td><td>Discuss potential future research directions with NCA, for example conducting studies in other parts of the theoretical domain to verify generalizability of the current findings,  or  applying NCA in  related substantive areas that could benefit from the necessity causal perspective.</td><td>Article: @kohler2023constructive</td></tr>

    </tbody>
</table>
</div>

<script>
  const publication = document.querySelector('#publication');
  const priority = document.querySelector('#priority');
  
  const table = new DataTable('#assesments', {
      info: false, 
      order: [[2, 'asc']],
      pageLength: 100,
      lengthMenu: [10, 25, 50, 100]
  });
  let publications = getSelectValues(publication);
  let priorities = getSelectValues(priority);
 
  table.search.fixed('range', function (searchStr, data, index) {
    if (JSON.stringify(publications) !== JSON.stringify(['All'])) {
        if (!publications.includes(data[0])) {
          return false;
        }
    }
    
    if (JSON.stringify(priorities) !== JSON.stringify(['All'])) {
        if (!priorities.includes(data[1])) {
          return false;
        }
    }
    
    return true;
  });
 
  publication.addEventListener('change', function () {
      publications = getSelectValues(publication);
      table.order([[2, 'asc']]);
      table.draw();
  });
  priority.addEventListener('change', function () {
      priorities = getSelectValues(priority);
      table.order([[2, 'asc']]);
      table.draw();
  });
  
  function getSelectValues(select) {
    var result = [];
    var options = select && select.options;
    for (let i=0; i < options.length; i++) {
      let opt = options[i];
      if (opt.selected) {
        result.push(opt.value || opt.text);
      }
    }
    
    if (result.includes('All') && result.length > 1) {
      result.shift();
      select.getElementsByTagName('option')[0].selected = '';
    }
    
    return result;
  }
</script>
